unlawful occupants, and provide the said report to the Municipal Accountant.

We also recommended and the Municipal Mayor agreed to:
a. institute an Appraisal Committee to appraise the value of the 103 unrecorded
parcels of land owned by the Municipality based on Tax Declarations;
b. After determining the nature of acquisition and its value, require the Municipal
Accountant to recognize in the Municipality’s books of accounts said one hundred
three (103) parcels of land using the appraised value, and make proper disclosure
in the Notes to the Financial Statements on their actual usage as validated by the
General Services Office;
c. require the Municipal Accountant to make adjusting entries for the noted
misclassification of accounts and overstatement due to properties not owned by
the Municipality. We also recommended that lots acquired in 2014 for the
barangays Gilerang Kawayan and Ilat North which were funded by both municipal
and barangay funds be recorded in barangay books at the full acquisition cost.

We recommended and the Management agreed that the Municipal Accountant promptly
record completed projects funded by trust funds so as not to misstate PPE balances
at a particular reporting period.

We recommended and the Management agreed that for ensuing years, the Municipal
Accountant transfer unexpended balances from the General Fund books to the Special
Trust Fund using the expense account “Transfer of Unspent Current Year DRRM Funds
to the Trust Funds (5-02-15-010)” in the General Fund books and income account
“Transfer from General Fund of Unspent DRRM to the Trust Fund” in the Trust Fund
books before closing the same.

- Administrator of Waterworks System to coordinate with Treasurer monitor
the unpaid billings and strictly enforce the administrative provisions of 2011
Revised Revenue Code;
- Municipal Treasurer to provide the Municipal Accountant the List of Delinquent
Waterworks System Fees with amount due and collectible and the latter to recognize
in the books of accounts the accrued income and subsequently follow the accrual
method of accounting in recording waterworks system fees

We recommended and the Management agreed that the Municipal Mayor and Municipal
Accountant endorse for reversion the long outstanding payables and immediately
pay/release the terminal leave benefits.

We recommended and the Municipal Mayor agreed to direct the officer in charge for
market operations to strictly enforce collection for the unpaid goodwill/royalties and
monthly rentals and strictly impose the provision stated in Section 1 of Title 13 and
Section 1 of Rule 3 of the 2017 Revised Market Code. Provide the Municipal
Accountant the List of Uncollected Monthly Stall Rental Fees and the latter to recognize
in the books of accounts the accrued income and subsequently follow the accrual
method of accounting in recording monthly market rental fees.

We recommended and the created Project Monitoring Committee ensure the timely implementation of the identified priority development
projects. We also recommended and the Management agreed that the Municipal
Planning and Development Officer to refrain from utilizing the fund for projects that do
not partake socio-economic development as guided by DILG and DBM Joint
Memorandum Circular No. 2017-1 dated February 22, 2017.

To be able to put the landfill in full operational status in the soonest time possible, we
recommend and the Management agreed that the Municipal Mayor:
- direct the Designated MENRO to prioritize the required permits and clearances;
and
- enjoin the Designated MENRO, MPDC, Municipal Budget and Municipal
Engineer to work hand in hand for the approval of the budget proposal for and
construction of the lacking structures/facilities of the Sanitary Landfill to
materialize the plans for its eventual operation.

We recommended and the Municipal Mayor agreed that the Municipal Development
Council clearly specify the detailed descriptions, locations and cost of priority
programs, projects and activities funded out of 20 per cent Development Fund and
ensure that such projects partake the nature of investment or capital expenditures. For
projects that are presented in generic terms and with lump-sum appropriation, secure
the approval of the Sangguniang Bayan before the implementation and disbursements
of the fund.

We recommended and the Municipal Mayor required the Disaster Risk Reduction and
Management Officer (LDRRMO) to prepare and submit to the local sanggunian through
the Local Disaster Risk Reduction and Management Council the annual LDRRMO Plan
and budget specifying under a separate caption the list of programs, activities and
projects or the intention for which the unexpended LDRRMF balances are to be used,
and timely implement projects to ensure that the purpose for which the fund was
established are met.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the Municipal Social Welfare and
Development Officer to update its master list indicating the economic status of each
senior citizen and select its beneficiaries in order of priority prescribed under DSWD
Administrative Order No. 15 series of. Likewise, conduct quarterly validation and
monitoring of the beneficiaries to determine their situation, ascertain their eligibility
and entitlement and to ensure optimal utilization of the stipend for the full
implementation of Social Pension Program of the government.

Foregoing considered, we recommended and the Municipal Mayor directed the GAD
Focal Person to continue to work on the gender analysis to concretely define its gender
issues, and accordingly design programs and activities that could most address them.

We recommended and the Municipal Mayor directed the Inventory Committee to
prioritize the conduct inventory and inspection for the unserviceable properties and
embody in an Inventory and Inspection Report of Unserviceable Property (IIRUP) and
facilitate their disposal of in accordance with the aforecited rules and regulations.

We recommended and the Municipal Mayor required the submission of a utilization
report in order to substantiate the actual use of the fund considering the deficiencies
noted as compared to the submitted proposals supporting the disbursement of the
Municipality.

We recommend and the Municipal Mayor and Municipal Human Resource Officer
agreed that upon completion of the Human Resource Management Plan, job orders will
be hired based on actual human resource needs for pieces of work that cannot be
provided by the existing manpower of the Municipality.

We also recommended and the Municipal Mayor required to stop acquiring services
of a legal consultant except in cases under par. 3.1 of Section 481 of RA 7160.

We recommended that Management require all Accountable Officers to settle their respective cash advances at the end of the year to reflect the correct balances of the accounts affected in the books of accounts of the Agency.  Henceforth, avoid granting subsequent cash advances to accountable officers with outstanding cash advances and withhold salaries and/or any amount due  to those Accountable Officers who were unable to comply with the pertinent rules and regulations on cash advances.  Adhere strictly to the guidelines in the granting, utilization and settlement of cash advances as enunciated in COA Circular No. 97-002 dated February 10, 1997.

Cause for the immediate liquidation of the outstanding cash advances amounting to ₱1.66 million as of December 31, 2018 and submit the liquidation report to the Auditor for post-audit. Send Demand Letters to the concerned Accountable Officer(s), officers and employees with unliquidated cash advances for monitoring purposes.

We reiterated our previous year’s audit recommendation that the agency strictly comply with the procedure and accounting treatment in the recording of Real Property Tax and Special Education tax as prescribed under Section 248 of R.A. 7160 and Sections 19 and 20 of the NGAS for Local Government Units, Volume I.

We recommended and reiterated that Management:
Convene the Inventory Committee, conduct the annual physical count of all its property, plant and equipment and submit the corresponding RPCPPE to the Office of the Auditor;
Evaluate the Agency’s property and supply management system, identify lapses therein and correct the same the soonest; and
Direct the Municipal Treasurer and Municipal Accountant to maintain the respective complete property records required from their offices and reconcile their records.
Strict adherence to existing laws, rules and regulations in the proper management of government properties is required.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive require:
The Office of the Municipal Accountant to submit to the Auditor the supporting documents of all accumulated accounts payable as reflected in the Schedule of Accounts Payable as of December 31, 2018   to back up the authenticity of the recorded obligations in the books of accounts of the Municipal Government;
Undertake in-depth evaluation to identify the veracity, nature and claim of the recorded payables both for the current and prior years ledgers/files to determine which are valid claims or fictitious accounts; and
Take up the appropriate reversion/adjustments in the books of the LGU for those established transactions with no valid claimants and with dormant accounts in order to have a fair presentation of accounts in the financial statements.

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant and the Municipal Treasurer promptly prepare the disbursement vouchers covering the remittances and supporting documents pertaining to the collections and prepare the checks covering the said remittances and transmit to the concerned agencies on or before the due date to avoid surcharges and penalties.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive instruct the Municipal Accountant to evaluate the lump-sum amount of various remittances from barangays and identify specific names of barangays with the corresponding amount due. After which, require the Municipal Accountant and Municipal Treasurer to immediately release the respective amount payable to the 22 barangays of the Municipal Government as well as to the Province of Masbate as mandated under the existing regulations.

Further, require the Offices of the Municipal Accountant and Municipal Treasurer to adhere strictly to the guidelines relative to the prompt release of the barangay shares from realty taxes as stated under Section 271 (d) of the Local Government Code and shares from CTCs collections in the ensuing years.

We recommended that the Offices of the three Municipal Economic Enterprises provide all the pertinent records of San Pascual Water System, San Pascual Local Electric Facility consumers/members and San Pascual Polytechnic College students to the Office of the Municipal Accountant for the latter to set up the receivable accounts and record its corresponding subsidiary ledgers in the books of accounts of the Municipal Government. Close coordination of the aforementioned Offices is further recommended to evaluate the appropriate amount of receivables from the consumers and students to be recorded in the books of the LGU in order to reflect the accurate balances of receivables and income accounts.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive reinforce personnel at the Office of the Municipal Accountant with appropriate skills to augment the required workforce and strengthen the Office of the Municipal Accountant in order to accomplish the required deliverables and meet the legal deadlines on reports/accounts submission. Further, require the Office of the Municipal Treasurer to immediately submit the paid vouchers/payrolls accounts and issued ORs to the Office of the Municipal Accountant for recording in the agency books of accounts, after which, the same must be submitted to the Auditor within the first 10 days of the ensuing month including their supporting documents pursuant to Section 107 of P.D. 1445 and COA Circular 2009-006.
It is suggested that the Office of the Municipal Treasurer submit the monthly accounts on or before the fifth day of the ensuing month to the Office of the Municipal Accountant to ensure compliance with the required deadlines.
Prompt submission of accounts and other mandated reports will facilitate timely review and verification of the agency’s financial transactions, thus corrective measures can be implemented, if necessary.

We recommended that the Municipal Planning and Development Office (MPDO) be required to prepare a well-planned/studied development programs/projects under the 20 per cent EDF that can be implemented immediately in order to optimize utilization and bring the desired benefits to its constituents and to avoid future reversion/realignment of appropriation. All development projects implemented by the Municipal Government be closely monitored by MPDO to ensure that government funds are utilized for the intended purpose. Progress/periodic reports must also be prepared to validate actual existence and accomplishment of programs/projects being implemented. Close coordination by the Municipal Engineer and MPDO must be made to effectively prioritize programs, projects and activities. Further, the LFC, as the Chief Fiscal Planner, must conduct thorough evaluation in the projection of income for every budget year that will surely support the planned PPAs of the agency.

We recommended that Management require:
The Municipal Accountant and the Municipal Treasurer to transfer the unutilized balance of the LDRRMF from the General Fund to the Trust Fund under the account Trust Liability-DRRM.  Ensure that the unexpended balance of QRF and LDRRMF-MOOE for each year be timely transferred at year-end with the corresponding cash back up in order to reflect the correct balance of the cash and liability accounts in the financial statements; and
The Accounting Office to coordinate with the Budget Office to reconcile their records and obtain the correct amount of the unexpended balances QRF, LDRRM-MOOE and LDRRMF-CO for the current year and previous years.

We recommended that Management require the MDRRMO, in close coordination with the MDRRMC, to prepare a well-planned/studied LDRRMFIP which must also include under a separate caption, the list of projects and activities charged to the unexpended LDRRMF of previous years which must be implemented as planned in order to optimize utilization and bring the desired benefits to its constituents and to avoid future reversion of the fund to the General fund.  Strict compliance with COA Circular No. 2012-002 and RA 10121 is required.

recommended the Municipal Mayor to instruct the Municipal Accountant to adjust the book reconciling items and to submit to the Office of the Auditor the original copies of the debit/credit memos issued by the depository banks as required under COA Circular 96-011. In order to facilitate verification of the validity of the entries made by the bank.
observations.managementAction: The Municipal Accountant claimed that no debit/credit memos were received for CY 2016 and that they only relied on the bank statements. But starting CY 2017, the Municipal Accountant requested for the debit/credit memos and copies of such were forwarded to the audit team.
observations.reason: Journal Entry Vouchers numbered 100-17-09-1948 to 1950 were prepared for the reversion of stale checks to Accounts Payable. The Municipal Accountant prepared the debit/credit memos and submitted to COA. The LBP refused to give the original debit/credit memos but submitted the authority letter to debit/credit account given to them by the Municipality.

observations.recommendation: recommended that the Municipal Mayor to: • require the Municipal Accountant to transfer the unexpended LDRRMF balance of the 70% Mitigation Fund- MOOE and the 30% Quick Response Fund for CY 2016 to the Special Trust Fund under the account Trust Liability-DRRM by year of transfer and properly monitor such balances due for reversion to general fund of transfer and prepare the necessary documentation to support such transfer. Likewise, prepare a justification for the discrepancy on the transferred unexpended balance and the suggested transfer based on the 2015 audit observation and recommendation. • require the Municipal Disaster Risk Reduction and Management Office (MDRRM) to submit the correct 5% LDRRMF Utilization Report and ensure that the succeeding plans and reports are properly reconciled with the reports submitted by the Municipal Accountant. • require the Municipal Disaster Risk Reduction and Management Office (MDRRM) to ensure that the future programs and activities charged to the 5% LDRRMF are aligned to the Disaster Prevention and Preparedness goals of the Municipality. Likewise, direct the Municipal Budget Officer to appropriate funds for the MOOE and Capital Outlay of the MDRRM Office to avoid charging non-priority expenditures to the calamity fund. • instruct the Municipal Accountant to ensure that the funds procured or received be recorded on their proper inventory accounts instead of directly charging to expense to effect proper monitoring and supervision on the issuance and distribution of such disaster related items.
observations.managementAction: By the end of 2018, non-priority expenditures were not charged against the 5% LDRRMF. The Municipal Accountant prepared the necessary documentation to transfer the unexpended LDRRMF balance for the 70% Mitigation Fund-MOOE and the 30% Quick Response Fund for CY 2016 to the Special Trust Fund. RPCPPE was submitted. The Municipal Disaster Risk Reduction and Management Office (MDRRM) submitted the correct utilization report and other supporting documents except for some documentary requirements. The Municipal Accountant and MDRRM were requested to adjust and reconcile. The Municipal Accountant ensured that supplies and goods are recorded as inventories prior to distribution.
observations.reason: The 2016 unexpended balance for CY 2016 had been transferred. Journal Entry Voucher was prepared and supporting documents were submitted. Some documentary requirements still to be complied for full reconciliation but the main transfer was effected and documentation prepared.

observations.recommendation: recommended that a feasibility study be made; that the Municipal Mayor instruct the concerned officials to have an in-depth study/review of the proposed site of the sanitary landfill in accordance with the provided criteria of Sec. 40 of RA 9003 prior to its establishment to comply with the order of the National Solid Waste Management Commission and DENR.
observations.managementAction: A feasibility study was made relative to the proposed site of the sanitary landfill in accordance with the criteria of Sec. 40 RA 9003. The Municipal Mayor instructed concerned officials to have an in-depth study/review.
observations.reason: A feasibility study was made and the DENR-EMB issued the ECC on June 24, 2018.

observations.recommendation: recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the Inventory Committee, after conducting complete physical count of Municipality’s PPE, prepare the corresponding report (RPCPPE) and reconcile any differences between accounting records and GSO records. Identify whether those properties were transfers, missing or unserviceable. Secure the necessary documents to support those PPEs to be able to reconcile with the Inventory Report of the GSO. Further, RPCPPE submitted is not reliable as stated above. We further recommended that the Inventory Committee and the Municipal Accountant indicate in the inventory report and PPE schedule the complete details/location of the PPE to substantiate its physical existence. Schedule the complete details/location of the PPE to substantiate its physical existence.
observations.managementAction: RPCPPE was prepared to identify whether those properties were existing, missing or unserviceable but does not reflect only existing items, hence submitted items that are recorded in the Inventory Report. The Municipal Mayor directed the Inventory Committee to exhaust all possible efforts of looking into the available files. After exhausting efforts no more records were retrieved of prior years’ purchases during the first twenty (20) years of operation of the Municipality. RPCPPE was submitted although not reliable as the details/location of the PPE were not presented in the submitted RPCPPE. The Municipal Accounting Department secured supporting documents for items where available and reconciled those items. Complete details/location of the PPE were submitted in parts.
observations.reason: Complete reconciliation between GSO and Accounting is still on process. Some PPE items from beginning balances could not be fully identified due to lack of original supporting documents and records for prior years’ acquisitions. Matter was not given priority by concerned office.

observations.recommendation: recommended that the Municipal Accountant to coordinate with the Provincial Accountant to reconcile the balances of the accounts Due from/Due to LGUs by identifying the causes of discrepancies in the corresponding subsidiary ledgers and to analyze the Due to and Due from Other Funds so that appropriate adjustments can be made on the affected accounts. Further, periodic reconciliation of the accounts should be made to ensure the accuracy of the recorded financial transactions.
observations.managementAction: Reconciliation was done by the Provincial Accounting and Treasury that led to a lower discrepancy. By the end of 2018, the discrepancy was reduced to ₱143,899.30. The Municipal Accountant coordinated with the Provincial Accountant for reconciliation of the balances.
observations.reason: Reconciliation was already made by the Provincial and Accounting Treasury but there is still an outstanding balance which is being followed up. The remaining discrepancy represents the beginning balances which is outstanding for quite a long time.

observations.recommendation: recommended that the Municipal Accountant and the Municipal Treasurer verify the existing difference between the respective records. Review the existence of tax delinquencies from previous years. Have a detailed reconciliation/comparison of the amounts from beginning balances, set up, collection and any recorded adjustments in order to locate where the discrepancies occur.
observations.managementAction: Reconciliation of RPT/SET was undertaken by Accounting and Treasury that resulted to a lower discrepancy. By year-end, the remaining discrepancy was reduced to ₱143,899.30. The Municipal Accountant and Municipal Treasury were coordinating and verifying records.
observations.reason: Reconciliation was done and discrepancy reduced but reconciliation not yet fully closed as of year-end due to differences in some beginning balances and collection adjustments; further detailed reconciliation still ongoing.

observations.recommendation: recommended that the Municipal Accountant and the Municipal Treasurer provide other explanation/clarification on how the Trust Fund incurred cash overdraft and ensure that the fund is utilized only according to the fund’s purpose.
observations.managementAction: The Municipal Accountant and Municipal Treasurer explained that the deficit pertains to receivables as reflected in the financial statements. Due from other LGUs of ₱81,463.20 and other receivables of ₱1,070,500.00 pertains to loans to KKK and other financial loans. The Municipal Accountant ensured that the fund is monitored and used per purpose.
observations.reason: The cash deficit was verified and the nature of receivables explained and proper monitoring ensured.

observations.recommendation: recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the Municipal Accountant to submit to the Office of the Auditor the original copies of debit/credit memos issued by the depository banks as required under COA Circular 96-011 in order to facilitate verification of the validity of the entries made by the bank.
observations.managementAction: The Municipal Accountant submitted copies of debit/credit memos to the audit team. The LBP refused to give the original copies but provided authority letter and copies. The Municipal Accountant coordinated with the depository banks to secure original supporting documents.
observations.reason: Copies of debit/credit memos were forwarded to the audit team. The Land Bank of the Philippines refused to give original memos but provided authority letter; matter considered addressed for audit purposes.

observations.recommendation: recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the concerned officials to: (a) coordinate with the respective agencies at GSIS, PhilHealth and PAG-IBIG to identify the unremitted balances and immediately remit the amounts due; (b) identify the instances of cause/s of remittance over remittance noted for some months and make the proper adjustments and (c) remit the due GSIS loan amortizations pursuant to Section 14.2, Rule III of the Implementing Rules and Regulations of RA 8291 to avoid the incurrence of unnecessary financial expenses in the form of imposed penalties.
observations.managementAction: Adjustment was done per JEV No. 100-18-03-0556 dated March 28, 2018 on the recorded over remittance on February and March 2017 loan amortizations. Reconciliation with GSIS, Pag-IBIG and PhilHealth was undertaken. Some causes of over remittance were identified; other instances still being identified. Remittance of GSIS loan amortizations were already made.
observations.reason: Reconciliation with agencies ongoing; some adjustments were made via journal entries but other causes of over remittances were not yet fully identified.

observations.recommendation: recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the Municipal Accountant and the Municipal Engineer to cause the submission of the lacking supporting documents enumerated therein aside from the documentary requirements, to ensure completeness of supporting documents for the advance and progress payments made for the construction/rehabilitation of public market before payment; the Municipal Engineer to compute actual liquidated damages for delays incurred and effect deduction from succeeding payments; the Municipal Accountant/Municipal Engineer to ensure collection/payment of under-computed retention money; and the Municipal Engineer to comply to COA Circular 2013-004 on the information and publicity of government programs/projects/activities.
observations.managementAction: The lacking supporting documents for advances and progress payments were submitted. The Municipal Engineer computed the required liquidated damages and explained that variation orders changed scope (roof to roof deck) thus liquidated damages were not charged. The 10% retention money was deducted and adjusted via DV No. 2018-07-1149 dated July 10, 2018. Signboards and required information were not posted timely in some cases but some were posted later.
observations.reason: Most lacking documents were submitted and journal entries made to correct entries. Some public information postings were not initially complied but later installed; one matter (signboard requirements) was not given full attention by the concerned office initially.

observations.recommendation: recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the MENRO, Municipal Engineer and the Municipal Accountant to submit the contract pertaining to the construction of sanitary landfill together with all the required documents specified in COA Circular 2009-001, to allow the Auditor to perform review of the contract, and for the Management to be able to institute appropriate action/s in case the required deficiencies are noted. Further, facilitate the issuance of Environmental Compliance Certificate (ECC) so as to substantiate the municipality’s compliance to environmental rules and regulations in the establishment of sanitary landfill and not to risk the government’s resources utilized for the said project. In case of non-approval of the ECC application, necessary action shall be taken against concerned officials and employees for allowing the expenditures and incurrence of P21,788,970.10.
observations.managementAction: The MENRO, Municipal Engineer and Municipal Accountant submitted the contract and related documents to COA. The Municipal Engineer facilitated the ECC application and the DENR-EMB issued the ECC on June 24, 2018.
observations.reason: ECC was issued June 24, 2018. The contract and required documents were submitted to COA, allowing review. No further action required.

observations.recommendation: recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct: a) the GAD Focal Person, SWM Officer, LDRRM Officer, Municipal Engineer and MPDO to submit justification why several non-GAD projects like garbage collection and disposal, rescue and response activities, drilling and installation of waterworks system, and others were cited among GAD activities in the GAD Plan and Budget; b) the GAD Focal Person to ensure that the programs/projects included in the GAD Plan are gender responsive; c) the GAD Focal Point System and Technical Working Group to strictly adhere to reporting requirements of GAD, certify compliance and communicate adjustments to the GAD Plan and Budget through written communication to DILG to properly monitor planned GAD programs/activities in accordance with the approved budgets.
observations.managementAction: Municipal GAD Focal Person and concerned offices provided explanations and submitted documentation. By year-end 2018, sex-disaggregated data from CBMS became available for planning 2019. Some clarifications on non-GAD items were provided. The municipality was able to derive sex-disaggregated data for 2019 planning. The GFPS worked on compliance and DILG evaluated and found compliant as of end-2018.
observations.reason: Sex-disaggregated data were derived from CBMS in 2019 and used for planning. Reporting requirements are being complied with and the GFPS actions were evaluated and found compliant by DILG.

observations.recommendation: recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the Municipal Accountant to transfer the unexpended balance of the QRF and the DRRM-MOOE to the Special Trust Fund under the account Trust Liability-DRRM for account by year and properly monitor balances for reversion to general fund. Likewise, ensure subsidiary ledger for Trust Liability is maintained by year of transfer and properly monitor balances due for reversion to general fund if unutilized after five years contrary to COA Circular No. 2012-002.
observations.managementAction: Journal Entry Voucher No. 100-18-02-034 dated February 27, 2018 was prepared to transfer the unexpended/unobligated balance to the Special Trust Fund account Trust Liability-DRRM. Subsidiary ledgers by year of transfer of unutilized DRRMF were maintained but not fully monitored for reversion amounts. Some adjustments were done.
observations.reason: The transfer was effected and journal entry prepared. However subsidiary ledgers were maintained but not consistently by year for determination of reversion amounts; monitoring and compliance with COA Circular 2012-002 on year-by-year subsidiary ledgers needs improvement.

observations.recommendation: recommended that the MPDC and Municipal Development Council coordinate with the Barangays to identify priority programs/projects relative to the 20% Development Fund; ensure the programs/projects are clearly specified in the budget and that MPDC should ensure that projects funded out of 20% Development Fund are investment or capital expenditures; and the Municipal Development Council to clearly specify the detailed descriptions, locations and cost of priority programs, projects and activities funded out of 20% Development Fund and ensure such projects partake the nature of investment or capital expenditures instead of lumpsum appropriations and secure Sanggunian approval as necessary.
observations.managementAction: Coordination with barangays through MPDC meeting held in August 6, 2018 identified priority projects and assistance required. For CY2019 AIP preparation and prior audit review, projects sourced from lump sum appropriations were identified and being regularized. A Project Monitoring Team was created.
observations.reason: Some projects were implemented, some ongoing, some not implemented; lump-sum appropriation issues were noted and two projects were procured without prior Sanggunian approval. Further compliance underway in CY2019.

observations.recommendation: recommended that: 1) Local School Board ensure that disbursements are supported with concrete proof of delivery including Acceptance Report with complete name and designation of the receiving school representative; 2) Bids and Awards Committee (BAC) to submit justification why the winning bidder was declared eligible when various eligibility requirements were not submitted during the bidding process and to monitor performance security posting and other procedural requirements; 3) Municipal Treasurer to issue demand letters to debtors to enforce collection and coordinate assistance from COMELEC for locating debtors; and 4) BAC to ensure posting to PhilGEPS and compliance with procurement posting requirements.
observations.managementAction: Copies of disbursement documents were provided and some missing attachments were collated. The BAC provided justifications and submitted required documents; some evidence on posting and performance security were provided and improvements in monitoring undertaken. Municipal Treasurer coordinated with COMELEC and Local Civil Registry for addresses but COMELEC refused to provide information citing Data Privacy; Municipal Treasurer sought assistance from Local Civil Registry. Some justifications and documents were submitted to COA.
observations.reason: Some required documents were submitted and improved controls instituted, but some issues remained such as inability to obtain COMELEC data and pending justifications from BAC for certain transactions. Full compliance pending.

observations.recommendation: recommended that the BAC submit supporting documents and justification for the award; the Municipal Accountant to make the appropriate journal entries to recognize assets arising from BUB Trust Fund to General Fund; and ensure posting of Notice of Award, Notice to Proceed and contract in PhilGEPS as required.
observations.managementAction: BAC provided copies of some supporting documents; Journal Entry Voucher No. 100-18-05-1021N dated May 21, 2018 was made to recognize assets arising from BUB Trust Fund to General Fund. The BAC attended seminars and planned posting to PhilGEPS; posting was to be made in CY2019.
observations.reason: Journal entries were made to recognize transferred PPE. Posting to PhilGEPS was pending at end of 2018 but measures were taken to comply.

observations.recommendation: recommended that the Mayor enjoin the BAC to encourage public competitive procurement instead of alternative modes and avoid splitting of procurement; ensure procurement documentation and justification are prepared where alternative modes are used.
observations.managementAction: The Municipality justified that purchases were of urgency warranting negotiated procurement instead of public bidding in some instances. For CY2018, 9 projects were procured through negotiated procurement and 2 projects were under procurement.
observations.reason: The BAC provided justifications and monitored procurement; documentation was provided as required and negotiated procurement used under urgency circumstances.

observations.recommendation: recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct concerned officials to refrain from processing claims for legal expenses unless supported by legal basis; that the amount of ₱168,320.00 be refunded to the municipality’s funds; disregard settlement of ₱31,680.00 offset from the irregular claim and retain it as a disallowance pending collection from the former public official; and require Municipal Accountant to correct related entries.
observations.managementAction: Journal Entry Voucher No. 100-18-04-1052 dated May 28, 2018 was drawn to correct entries on the refund of claims of legal expenses. The Municipal Mayor responded that they will take action to seek refund; internal audit noted deficiencies in the liquidation submitted by claimants.
observations.reason: Entries were corrected via JEV but full refund from the claimant had not been completed and related administrative actions were pending.

observations.recommendation: recommended that the Municipal Engineer and concerned officials submit lists of all ongoing government projects and those to be implemented during the year in compliance with COA Circular 2013-004 for reporting and validation.
observations.managementAction: Lists of ongoing projects and those to be implemented were submitted at the beginning of the year in compliance with the regulation. The Municipal Engineer submitted required reports for validation.
observations.reason: Required project lists and reports were submitted to COA and validation undertaken; timely reporting improved.

observations.recommendation: recommended comply with COA Circular No. 2013-004 and DILG Memorandum Circular No. 2010-101 on the required form of signage, tarpaulins, posting of names and photos of top officials and other publicity requirements.
observations.managementAction: Upon inspection, tarpaulins and signboards were installed and pictures, names and initials of concerned officials were displayed in most projects; some installations were done after audit findings and some remain to be fully updated.
observations.reason: Some signage and publicity were installed after audit; some projects still lacked complete public posting as required.

observations.recommendation: recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct concerned offices to allocate the 1% IRA for LCPC and prepare the LDPC and LIPC to identify programs/projects and activities for children, and ensure reporting and monitoring of LCPC activities.
observations.managementAction: The Municipality reported that 1% IRA allocation was used for LCPC related activities such as youth day care operations and child protection related activities amounting to ₱1,140,537.72; the Sanggunian was informed of PPAs that can be funded by the 1% IRA. The Municipality was awarded child friendly local government recognition and prepared LCPC related plans. Activities were implemented.
observations.reason: Some LCPC activities were funded and implemented; however the formal LDPC and LIPC documentation and the explicit 1% IRA allocation process required by DILG Circular were not fully formalized within the year.

observations.recommendation: recommended the Municipal Mayor to ensure issuance of supporting documents for deliveries, require BAC to justify awards and monitor postings and to direct the Municipal Treasurer to issue demand letters to enforce collection and coordinate with COMELEC for locating debtors.
observations.managementAction: Documentary requirements were collated and provided. BAC submitted some justifications and was instructed to monitor performance security posting. Municipal Treasurer coordinated with COMELEC but could not obtain data due to data privacy; other avenues like Local Civil Registry were pursued. BAC was instructed to post required notices.
observations.reason: Some documentary deficiencies were addressed; however COMELEC data was not accessible and some BAC justifications remained outstanding; collection enforcement continues.

observations.recommendation: recommended that BAC post notices and contracts in PhilGEPS and the Municipal Accountant recognize assets arising from trust-funded projects.
observations.managementAction: Journal Entry Voucher No. 100-18-05-1021N dated May 21, 2018 was made to recognize assets arising from trust-funded projects. BAC attended seminars and arranged postings to PhilGEPS to be completed in CY2019.
observations.reason: Recognition of assets completed via journal entry; posting to PhilGEPS was scheduled and actions were taken though actual posting deferred to CY2019.

observations.recommendation:
We reiterated the previous years’ audit recommendations that the concerned Agency Officials must strictly abide to the above-cited guidelines on cash advances. Also, require the Acting Municipal Accountant to stop the practice of granting cash advances to officials and employees who have outstanding balances. The Acting Municipal Accountant must see to it that all outstanding balance of cash advances are fully liquidated every end of the year and ensure that the corresponding liquidations are recorded to their appropriate accounts in order to present fairly the assets and expenses of the municipality.
Cause for the immediate liquidation of the outstanding cash advances amounting to Ᵽ1,870,200.30 as of December 31, 2017 and submit the liquidation report to the Auditor for post-audit. Send Demand Letters to the concerned Accountable Officer(s) with unliquidated cash advances. Appropriate legal action against those erring officials/employees must be initiated for continued non-compliance with the existing laws and regulations, if warranted.
observations.managementAction: Cash Advances balance was partially settled.(See related finding-Audit Observation No. 1)
observations.reason: (Please see related finding-Audit Observation No. 1)

observations.managementAction:
The Office of the Municipal Treasurer was only able to submit a mere partial listing of LGU’s properties as of December 31, 2018.
(Restated finding under Audit Observation No. 3)
observations.reason:
The agency has not created Inventory Committee. 
(Please see related finding-Audit Observation No. 3)

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that Management require the Municipal Treasurer and Municipal Accountant to strictly comply with the requirements with respect to the prompt submission of the monthly reports, trial balance and other financial reports to acquaint the chief executive of what happened in the agency and for early disclosure of accountability to improve performance efficiency. Prompt submission of these reports are required for early detection of errors for a sound financial position of the municipality.
Institute appropriate disciplinary action against those officials/employees who were responsible for the delayed preparation and/or non-submission of the required financial reports within the prescribed period, if warranted.
observations.managementAction: The Office of the Municipal Accountant had exerted efforts to partially comply with the submission timelines.  (Restated Audit Observation No. 8) .
observations.reason: Still not within the required timelines. Please see related finding-Audit Observation No. 8 for details.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that Management direct the officials concerned to ensure that the 20% of the IRA is optimally utilized in the prompt implementation of programs and projects to achieve the desirable socio-economic development. Strict observance of timetable for the implementation of specific phase of activities to be undertaken will aid the agency in patronizing activities to be implemented given the available resources for that period to achieve results.


Implement immediately the programs and projects relative to the unutilized balance of Ᵽ19,733,789.74 as embodied in the Annual Investment Plan.
observations.managementAction: For CY 2018, out of the total appropriation of ₱28,000,000.00 under the 20% Development Fund, the municipality has obligated ₱4,075,750.00 or 14.56% utilization only. Restated finding (Please see Audit Observation No. 9).
observations.reason: Poor monitoring of targeted plans as against actual implementation of PPAs as projected. Please see related finding-Audit Observation No. 9 for details

observations.recommendation: We recommended that Management direct the Treasurer and Accountant to transfer all unutilized balances of the LDRRMF to the TF bank account and record the transfer in the TF books. See to it that the fund must be incorporated in the LDRRMFIP.
observations.managementAction: The CY 2017 LDRRMF unexpended balance was transferred to Trust Fund.
observations.reason: The LGU is still unable to transfer the CY 2018 LDRRMF unexpended balance to Trust Fund. See related Audit Observation  No. 10.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the GAD Focal Point System must develop and maintain a GAD Database and regularly update the same as sources of relevant information for a systematic planning, programming and policy formulation in order to address the priority gender issues; review and conduct gender analysis of the PPAs to be implemented to determine which among are responsive to the identified gender issues and feasible for implementation; and establish a monitoring and evaluation system to periodically assess the GAD related accomplishments and significant concerns.
Further, ensure that the DILG, Regional Office issue the Certificate of Endorsement and effect its comments/and recommendation/s on the submitted LGU GAD Plan and Budget (GPB) and GAD Accomplishment Report (GAD AR) to see to it that it is gender responsive and have addressed the priority needs relative to the prevailing GAD concerns in the locality in accordance with the “Guide in the Review and Endorsement of LGU GAD Plan and Budget (GPB) and Review of GAD Accomplishment Report (GAD AR)” as required under JMC NO. 2016-01 by PCW, DILG, DBM and NEDA.
observations.managementAction: The agency was able to submit the GAD reports to PPDO & DILG for review. However, no certificate of approval was secured from the DILG.
observations.reason: The GAD Focal Point System of the municipality is not strengthened which resulted in poor monitoring and evaluation of the GAD PPAs if it had addressed the identified gender issues/concerns in the locality. Further, we have noted the delayed submission of the GAD Accomplishment Report thus, precluded the Audit Team from conducting verification.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Management require the Municipal Treasurer to deposit immediately and in full all undeposited collections as of December 31, 2016 with the authorized government depository bank. Require the collectors to remit their collections daily to avoid incurrence of shortages and/or possible misuse of public funds and refrain from releasing additional accountable forms unless previous ones were fully accounted for. Strict adherence to the abovementioned rules and regulations relative to the collections and deposits is required. We suggested that the prescribed Report of Collections and Deposits (RCD) from be utilized and be properly accomplished. Collections and deposits must be reported simultaneously in the same RCD to facilitate recording and monitoring of cash accountabilities. Henceforth, prepare and submit regularly the MRAAF and abstract of general and rally tax collections.
observations.managementAction: The balance of collections as of December 31, 2016 was deposited in CY 2017.
observations.reason: The Cash Local Treasury account  has a balance of Ᵽ103,762.39 as of December 31, 2018 which remained undeposited at year-end. The Municipal Treasurer was unable to strictly adhere to the existing regulations pertaining timelines in the deposit of government collections.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Management enforce all accountable officers to settle all their outstanding cash advances at the end of the year to reflect the correct balances of the accounts affected in the financial statements. Henceforth, avoid granting subsequent cash advances to accountable officers with unliquidated cash advances and withhold salaries and/or any amount due those accountable office who failed to comply with rules and regulations in the granting, utilization and settlement of cash advances as enunciated in COA Circular No. 97-002 dated February 10, 1997. Further, we recommended that the Local Chief Executive instruct the Office of the Municipal Accountant to immediately submit all liquidation reports and the corresponding supporting documents on the recorded settlement of cash advances granted to avoid suspension or disallowance in audit.
observations.managementAction: The agency has decreased the cash advances balance from Ᵽ1,978,965.28 for CY 2016 to ₱1,657,791.12 as of December 31, 2018.
observations.reason: Restated finding-Please see related Audit Observation No. 1.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the Local Chief Executive direct the Office of the Municipal Accountant to submit to the Auditor the supporting documents together with the schedule of all accumulated accounts payable as of December 31, 2016 to back up the authenticity of the recorded obligations in the books of accounts of the municipal government. In-depth evaluation must be undertaken to identify the veracity, nature and claim of the recorded payables both for the current and prior year ledgers/files to determine which are valid claims or fictitious accounts. Moreover, for those established transactions with no valid claimants and dormant accounts, the Acting Municipal Accountant must take up the appropriate reversion/adjustments in the books of the LGU for fair presentation of accounts in the financial statements.
Likewise, we recommended that the Management require the Municipal Accountant to undertake evaluation of payables to be set-up if duly supported with valid/actual claims and refrain from recording payables which are considered blind obligation. Ensure that no obligation has been certified as accounts payable unless backed up by sufficient evidence.
observations.managementAction: Please see Audit Observation No.  4 (Restated Finding).
observations.reason: However, the corresponding supporting documents/vouchers of the total accounts payable accounts as of December 31, 2018 were not presented to the Audit Team. Review of accounting records showed that no evaluation has been undertaken to establish the validity of claims on the recorded accounts payable particularly the prior years carried forward balances. Hence, the account was not appropriately sanitized to present fairly in the financial statements.

observations.recommendation: We recommended following measures, to wit: a) to require the Municipal Treasurer and the Chairman, Property Inventory Committee to prioritize the proper conduct of inventory taking of agency assets and to come up with a reconciled Report of Physical Count of Property, Plant and Equipment and a copy be furnished the Auditor to facilitate the verification and audit of the PPE accounts. Moreover, update and reconciliation of property records of each and every department is suggested to facilitate the task as well as to establish if there are missing properties or unserviceable properties still carried in the books of accounts of the agency; b) to require the OIC Municipal Accountant to submit the schedule/details of the computation for the monthly depreciation expense of the property, plant and equipment in order to check the correct balance of the account as presented in the year-end financial statements. Strict adherence to the accounting policies and procedures relative to the costing of property, plant and equipment is required; and c) to require the OIC Municipal Accountant to effect the recording of the procured assets totaling Ᵽ111,176.00 including the provision of the depreciation expense. Thorough review and verification of all the transactions of the Municipality before approving/recording the same to ensure the correctness of the classification of accounts as well as the accuracy of the accounting entries in accordance with the provisions of the Manual of the New Government Accounting System, Volume III must be done. Further, require the OIC Municipal Accountant to introduce measures to enhance the supervision of the accounting staff and ensure transactions are carefully analyzed. Make the necessary adjustments in the books for the misclassified accounts.
observations.managementAction: The agency was able  to prepare the subsidiary records of some PPE items only.
observations.reason:
The agency has not created Inventory Committee. 
Restated finding-Please see related Audit Observation No. 3.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that Management require the Municipal Treasurer to record the trust receipts for the downloaded funds from various national government agencies in the Trust Fund related reports, such as RCI, RCD, Cash Books for specific fund received, etc. Likewise, require the Office of the Municipal Accountant to record the trust receipts related transactions under the Trust Fund books of accounts including the maintenance of subsidiary ledgers for each specific downloaded fund per project/program. We would like to emphasize that trust funds must only be used exclusively for the specific purpose to which it was created of for which it came to the possession of the

local government and Sections 2. And 3 of COA Circular No. 92-382 dated July 3, 1992 must strictly be adhered to.
observations.managementAction: The Offices of the Mun. Accountant & Mun. Treasurer were able to record various downloaded funds from other NGAs as Trust Funds.
observations.reason: However, subsidiary ledgers for each specific downloaded fund per project/program was not maintained hence there is risk that it may be used to other purposes and not exclusively for the specific purpose released by the donor agency.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that Management require the Municipal Treasurer to immediately submit the above-mentioned lacking/missing Disbursement Vouchers including their supporting documents to the Office of the Municipal Accountant for recording in the agency books of accounts. Make necessary adjustments under the Cash in Bank account, if accounts are already submitted coupled with the verification in the bank statements for reconciliation purposes. We suggested that the Management evaluate their current internal control system in the actual procedure of handling the check disbursement transactions particularly on the custodial functions / release of the disbursement vouchers with their complete supporting documents at the Office of the Municipal Treasurer to ensure that all accounts/documents are forwarded to the Office of the Municipal Accountant on the required deadline to prevent further occurrence of this deficiency in the succeeding years. The management and the concerned officials and employees were also reminded of the administrative or criminal liability, under Section 39 (1) (3) of PD No. 1445 and Article 218 of the Revised Penal Code, which may be imposed as a consequence of their failure or refusal to render accounts without justifiable cause.
observations.managementAction: The Office of the Mun. Accountant had exerted efforts to partially comply with the submission of the CY 2016 accounts.
observations.reason: The agency was not able to submit the prior years unsubmitted accounts per demand letter sent.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the Chairperson, GAD Focal Point System promptly prepare and submit the agency’s GAD Plan and Budget (GPB) to the Provincial Planning and Development Office (PPDO) within the month of January to February of each year to see to it that the GPB of the city is aligned with the priorities of the provincial government. Likewise, from the PPDO the GPB must be forwarded to the DILG Regional office within the month of March of each year for appropriate review and approval. The certificate of approval rom the latter will ensure that the programs/projects/activities reflected in the GPB are in accordance with the provisions of the PCW/DILG/DBM/NEDA Join Memorandum Circular No. 2013-001 and other existing regulations/guidelines.
Furthermore, we suggested that the corresponding Annual GAD Accomplishment Report be also submitted to the DILG for the corresponding assessment of the reported PPAs undertaken ad for its consolidation to be reported to the Office of the PCW.
observations.managementAction: The agency was able to submit the GAD reports to PPDO & DILG for review. However, no certificate of approval was secured from the DILG.
observations.reason: The municipal government was not able to secure a Certificate of Approval from the DILG, Reg. Office to ensure that the programs/projects/activities reflected in the GPB are in accordance with the provisions of JMC No. 2016-001.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that at least 5% of the Annual Appropriation of the municipal government be allocated/appropriated for Gender and Development (GAD) Fund of each year as provided under Section 35 of the General Appropriations Act of 2016 and Section IV (a) (1) of COA Circular No. 2014-001, to ensure optimum benefits for the targeted beneficiaries in the implementation of the GAD related Programs, Projects and Activities (PPAs).
observations.managementAction: For CY 2017, the LGU has a total Annual Appropriations of Ᵽ149,792,195.90. On the other hand, evaluation of the submitted Annual GAD Plan and Budget for CY 2017 disclosed that the LGU has appropriated Ᵽ6,700,000.00 or 4.47% for mainstreamed GAD related PPAs thru the different implementing offices of the LGU.
observations.reason: The agency still fall short of the required minimum 5% appropriation for Gender and Development (GAD) Fund.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that Management direct the Accountant to prepare the Report on the Sources and Utilization of the DRMMF and the LDRRM Officer to submit a copy therefore to the Auditor on or before the 15th day after the end of each month.
observations.managementAction: The LGU was able to partially submit the DRRMF monthly report.
observations.reason: The MDRRMO was not able to observe the required reporting timelines.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that Management require the Municipal Treasurer and OIC Municipal Accountant to immediately submit the Disbursement Accounts and its supporting documents pertaining to the implemented projects under the GPB/BUB projects as well as the Memoranda of Agreement entered into by and between the Source Agencies and Implementing Agency (LGU San Pascual) and Fund Utilization Report duly received by the Donor Agency.
Further, we recommended that the LGU strictly comply with the rules and guidelines for the implementation, monitoring and reporting of the status of Grassroots Participatory Budgeting (GPB) or Bottom-Up Budgeting (BUB) Projects so that the purposes for which the same are established be efficiently and effectively attained.
observations.managementAction: The LCE instructed the concerned officials to submit the required lacking documents. The BAC Secretariat Office was tasked to coordinate with the concerned offices to partially comply the documentations pertaining to some project implemented by the LGU in CY 2017.
observations.reason: The Audit Team noted some deficiencies relative to the implementation of various PPAs of the LGU in CY 2017.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that Section 13 of IRR of RA No. 9184 be strictly observed to enhance the transparency of the procurement process and


in order to promote the ideals of good governance of the state including the local government units.
