Otherwise, revert to surplus account the payables, Due to LGUs-Others of P118,607.81, which have been outstanding for more than 10 years and consider as invalid/undocumented obligation, as required  under Section 98 of P.D. 1445.
secure proper documentation before effecting the necessary adjusting entries for erroneous recording/inclusion in the Due to/Due from LGUs account to ensure that the amounts are valid and conform to the rules and regulations that will cover such adjustments.

the Inventory Committee headed by the Municipal GSO to prepare inventory list by category and perform the actual and complete physical count of PPE in order to determine the existence of the recorded PPE accounts, reconcile the results of the count with the accounting records and establish the causes of discrepancies, whether items had already been missing, donated, sold and unserviceable to arrive at the correct balance in the Financial Statements.
the Accounting Department to exhaust all efforts to fully identify the PPEs included in the beginning/unidentified balances. Secure the necessary documents to support those PPEs to be able to reconcile with the Inventory Report of the GSO
the Chairman of the Committee on Disposal from the General Services Office to initiate/facilitate the immediate disposal of those unserviceable properties in accordance with the aforementioned rules and regulations prescribed per COA Circular No. 92-386 dated October 20, 1992 in order not to expose those properties to elements that cause further deterioration or loss of salvaged value and to enable the municipality to efficiently use/utilize the space occupied by these unserviceable properties. Likewise, to instruct the Municipal Accountant to make the necessary adjusting entries in the books of accounts upon disposal of the said property.

We recommended the Municipal Mayor to instruct the following:
Municipal Accountant to submit the documents pertaining to CY 2016 payables reflected in the Accounts Payable and Due to Officers and Employees account.
The Municipal Accountant to review the validity of the unpaid obligations or accounts payable which are outstanding for more than two years. And if found to have no valid claims against the local government unit, cause the reversion of the same to the unappropriated surplus.
Municipal Accountant and Municipal Treasurer to settle those obligations/ payables to legitimate creditors who have satisfactorily performed/delivered infrastructure, goods and services to the Municipality and whose claims are found valid and supported with all the necessary documents.

Based on the foregoing observations, we recommended the Municipal Mayor to:
require the Municipal Accountant to prepare the journal entry for the transfer of the unexpended LDRRMF balance of the 70% Mitigation Fund- MOOE and the 30% Quick Response Fund for CY 2016 to the proper Trust Liability account. Also, prepare the necessary documentation to support such transfer. Likewise, prepare a justification for the discrepancy on the transferred CY 2015 unexpended balance and the suggested transfer based on the 2015 audit observation and recommendation.
require the Municipal Disaster Risk Reduction and Management Office (MDRRM) to submit the correct 5% LDRRMF Utilization Report and ensure that the succeeding plans and reports are properly reconciled with the reports submitted by the Municipal Accountant.
require the Municipal Disaster Risk Reduction and Management Office (MDRRM) to ensure that the future programs and activities charged to the 5% LDRRMF are aligned to the Disaster Prevention and Preparedness goals of the Municipality. Likewise, direct the Municipal Budget Officer to appropriate funds for the MOOE and Capital Outlay of the MDRRM Office to avoid charging non-priority expenditures to the calamity fund.
instruct the Municipal Accountant to ensure that inventories procured or received be recorded on their proper inventory accounts instead of directly charging to expense to effect proper monitoring and supervision on the issuance and distribution of such disaster related items.

We recommended the Municipality to strictly observe provisions of Article 210 to 213 and 215 of RA 7160 and Section 3.4 of National Budget Circular No. 563 April 22, 2016.  Contributions and expenses related to leagues which are not included in the preceding articles are not government expenses and should not be charged against the funds of the municipality. Require the submission of the missing Disbursement Voucher and its supporting documents paid to PICPA under check no. 52407837 amounting to ₱1,500.00.  Likewise, ensure that only those annual dues and membership contributions were to be charged to the Membership Dues and Contributions to Organizations account to attain accuracy and fair presentation of the financial statements.

We recommended the Municipal Mayor to instruct the CBMS TWG to:
provide a justification for the incurrence of the delay in the implementation of Module I and II of the CBMS implementation.
ensure that the succeeding project activities will be accomplished within the target date of implementation. See to it that full compliance to work and financial plans are strictly observed to avoid delays in the implementation.

We recommended the Municipal Mayor to instruct the GAD Focal Person to:
properly monitor and report regularly the status of implementation of the activities stated in the Gender and Development Plan in order to address the gender issues. Ensure that the programs and projects included  in the GAD Plan are  gender responsive processes, procedures are properly followed and other factors that would affect the implementation must be considered to avoid problems when it comes to implementation
align the GAD Plans, Budget and its utilization only for the program, projects and activities which address gender issues and concerns for the successful achievement of GAD.
update the GAD Database to have an accurate basis that will strengthen the planning and budgeting processes for GAD and ensure that planned PPAs address identified gender issues, that objectives, performance indicators and targets are well-formulated to facilitate monitoring and evaluation for the attainment of GAD objectives.

We recommend that the Municipal Development Council ensure that the Annual Investment Plan constitutes the LGU’s priority programs, projects and activities (PPAs) to be integrated into the annual budget.  Also avoid changes of plan that will affect the realization of the Municipality’s goals as set forth in their approved Local Development Investment Program.  Likewise, the Municipal Mayor monitors  the immediate implementation of the projects that had been allocated so that full benefits of the project to the concern beneficiaries could be attain at the soonest possible time.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the following:
the Municipal Solid Waste Management Board
to conduct more intense information campaign to encourage full participation and compliance of all the Barangays in promoting and supporting the SWM programs of the Municipality;
to enact ordinances  necessary enforcing compliance particularly to waste segregation and proper disposal of waste and imposing appropriate penalties for non-cooperation;
to strictly enforce the municipal ordinances and resolutions issued pertaining to environmental compliance; and
to require the person in charge to closely monitor the hauling trips by consistently indicating the time of pick up, whether the truck is at full capacity or not, and have the waste collectors sign on the logbook. Likewise, indicate on the opposite of the transaction a column for the monitoring of billing/payment.  Prepare a summary report of the monitored number of trips of the dump trucks per day indicating the number of cubic meters of wastes hauled.
The Municipal Accountant, Bids and Awards Committee and other concerned officials to submit all the required documents found lacking, and the necessary clarifications/justifications for the deficiencies noted to substantiate the payments made.
the Municipal Accountant to verify the accuracy of the billings for hauling of wastes and ensure the completeness of supporting documents prior to processing of payments. Likewise, to immediately submit the supporting documents for the payable to Bauan Solid Waste Management amounting to ₱132,396.00.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the concerned officials to have an in-depth study/review of the proposed site of the sanitary landfill in accordance with the provided criteria of Sec. 40 of RA 9003 prior to its establishment to comply with the order of the National Solid Waste Commission and DENR.

We recommended that the Management require the Municipal Treasurer to deposit
immediately and in full all undeposited collections as of December 31, 2016 with the
authorized government depository bank. Reguire the collectors to remit their collections
daily to avoid incurrence of shortages and/or possible misuse of public funds and refrain
from releasing additional accountable forms unless previous ones were fully accounted for.
Strict adherence to the abovementioned rules and regulations relative to the collections and
deposits is required.

We suggested that the prescribed Report of Collections and Deposits (RCD) form be
utilized and be properly accomplished. Collections and deposits must be reported
simultaneously in the same RCD to facilitate recording and monitoring of cash
accountabilities. Henceforth, prepare and submit regularly the MRAAF and abstract of
general and realty tax collections.

We recommended that the Management enforce all accountable officers to settle all
their outstanding cash advances at the end of the year to reflect the correct balances of the
accounts affected in the financial statements. Henceforth, avoid granting subsequent cash
advances to accountable officers with unliquidated cash advances and withhold salaries
and/or any amount due those accountable officers who failed to comply with rules and
regulations in the granting, utilization and settlement of cash advances as enunciated in CO4
Circular No. 97-002 dated February 10, 1997.

Further, we recommended that the Local Chief Executive instruct the Office of the
Mamicipal Accountant to immediately submit all liquidation reports and the corresponding
supporting documents on the recorded settlement of cash advances granted to avoid
suspension or disallowance in qudit.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the Municipal Assessor and
Municipal Treasurer to immediately submit the complete Assessment Roll and RPTAR to the
OIC Municipal Accountant in order to facilitate the setting-up of RPT and SET receivables
account. Likewise, prior year’s collectibles and collections thereto be kept updated through
the maintenance of subsidiary ledger cards to ensure the reliability of the account balance of
Real Property Tax and Special Education Tax Receivables and the corresponding Deferred
Income accounts.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive direct the Qffice of the Municipal
Accountant to submit to the Auditor the supporting documents together with the schedule of
all accumulated accounts payable as of December 31, 2016 to back up the authenticity of
the recorded obligations in the books of accounts of the municipal government. In-depth
evaluation must be undertaken to identify the veracity, nature and claim of the recorded
payables both for the current and prior years ledgers/files to determine which are valid
claims or fictitious accounts. Moreover, for those established iransactions with no valid
claimants and dormant accounts, the Acting Municipal Accountant  must take up the
appropriate reversion‘adjustments in the books of the LGU for fair presentation of accounts
in the financial statements.

Likewise, we recommended that the Management require the Municipal Accountant
to undertake evaluation of payables to be set-up if duly supported with valid/actual claims
and refrain from recording payables which are considered blind obligation. Ensure that no
obligation has been certified as accounts payable unless backed up by sufficient evidence.

We recommended to require the Municipal Treasurer and the Chairman, Property
Inventory Committee to prioritize the proper conduct of inventory taking of agency assets
and to come up with a reconciled Report of Physical ~ Count of Property, Plant and
Equipment and a copy be furnished the Auditor to facilitate the verification and audit of the
PPE accounts. Moreover, update and reconciliation of property records of each and every
department is suggested to facilitate the task as well as to establish if there are missing
properties or unserviceable properties still carried in the books of accounts of the agency.

We recommended that the Agency require the OIC Municipal Accountant to submit
the schedule/details of the computation for the monthly depreciation expense of the property,
plant and equipment in order to check the correct balance of the account as presented in the
year-end financial statements. Strict adherence to the accounting policies and procedures
relative to the costing of property, plant and equipment is required.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive require the OIC Municipal
Accountant to effect the recording of the procured assets lotaling P111,176.00 including the
provision of the depreciation expense. Thorough review and verification of all the
transactions of the Municipality before approving/recording the same to ensure the
correctness of the classification of accounts as well as the accuracy of the accounting entries
in accordance with the provisions of the Manual of New Government Accounting System,
Volume IIl must be done. Further, require the OIC Municipal Accountant to introduce
measures to enhance the supervision of the accounting staff and ensure transactions are
carefully analyzed. Make the necessary adjustments in the books for the misclassified
accovnts.

We recommended that Management require the Municipal Treasurer to record the
trust receipts for the downloaded funds from various national government agencies in the
Trust Fund related reports, such as RCI, RCD, Cash Books for specific fund received, etc.
Likewise, require the Office of the Municipal Accountant to record the trust receipts related
transactions under the Trust Fund books of accounts including the maintenance of subsidiary
ledgers for each specific downloaded fund per project/program.

We would like to emphasize that trust funds must only be used exclusively for the
specific purpose to which it was created or for which it came to the possession of the local
government and Sections 2.b and 3 of COA Circular No. 92-382 dated July 3, 1992 must
strictly be adhered to.

We recommended that the Management require the Municipal Treasurer to
immediately submit the above-mentioned lacking/missing Disbursement Vouchers including
their supporting documents to the Office of the Municipal Accountant for recording in the
agency books of accounts. Make the necessary adjustments under the Cash in Bank account,

52
if accounts are already submitted coupled with the verification in the bank statements Jor
reconciliation purposes.

We suggested that the Management evaluate their current internal control system in
the actual procedure of handling the check disbursement transactions particularly on the
custodial functionsirelease of the disbursement vouchers with their complete supporting
documents at the Office of the Municipal Treasurer to ensure that all accounts/documents
are forwarded to the Office of the Municipal Accountant on the required deadline to prevent
Jurther occurrence of this deficiency in the succeeding years.

The management and the concerned officials and employees were also reminded of
the administrative or criminal liability, wnder Section 39 (1) (3) of PD No. 1445 and Article
218 of the Revised Penal Code, which may be imposed as a consequence of their failure or
refusal to render accounts without justifiable cause.

We recommended that the Chairperson, GAD Focal Point System prompily prepare
and submit the agency's GAD Plan and Budget (GPB) to the Provincial Planning and
Development Office(PPDO) within the month of January to February of each year to see to it
that the GPB of the city is aligned with the priorities of the provincial government. Likewise,
Jfrom the PPDO the GPB must be forwarded to the DILG Regional Office within the month of
March of each year for appropriate review and approval. The certificate of approval from
the latter will ensure that the programs/projects/activities reflected in the GPB are in
accordance with the provisions of the PCW/DILG/DBM/NEDA Joint Memorandum Circular
No. 2013-001 and other existing regulations/guidelines.

Furthermore, we suggested that the corresponding Annual GAD Accomplishment
Report be also submitted to the DILG for the corresponding assessment of the reported PPAs
undertaken and for its consolidation to be reported to the Office of the PCW.

We recommended that at least 5% of the Annual Appropriation of the municipal
government be allocated/appropriated for Gender and Development (GAD) Fund of each
year as provided under Section 35 of the General Appropriations Act of 2016 and Section IV
(@ (1) of COA Circular No. 2014-001, to ensure optimum benefits for the targeted
beneficiaries in the implementation of the GAD related Programs, Projects and Activities
(PPAs).

We recommended that Management direct the Accountant to prepare the Report on
the Sources and Utilization of the DRRMF and the LDRRM Officer to submit a copy
therefore to the Auditor on or before the 15" day after the end of each month.

We recommended that Management require the Municipal Treasurer and OIC
Municipal Accountant to immediately submit the Disbursement Accounts and its supporting
documents pertaining to the implemented projects under the GPB/BUB projects as well as
the Memoranda of Agreement entered into by and between the Source Agencies and
Implementing Agency (LGU San Pascual) and Fund Utilization Report duly received by the
Donor Agency.

Further, we recommended that the LGU strictly comply with the rules and guidelines
Jor the implementation, monitoring and reporting of the status of Grassroots Participatory
Budgeting (GPB) or Bottom-Up Budgeting (BUB) Projects so that the purposes for which the
same are established be efficiently and effectively attained.

We recommended that Section 13 of IRR of RA No. 9184 be sirictly observed to
enhance the transparency of the procurement process and in order to promote the ideals of
good governance of the state including the local government units.

We suggested that the office of the Bids and Awards Committee (BAC) revisit the
provisions  regarding  the  appropriate  Observers on  specific  type of
programs/projects/activities to be bid out to enhance the procurement process of the agency.
Moreover, the BAC Secretariat be well acquainted with the necessary documentary
requirements for the auditorial and technical review of all contracts entered into by the
Local Government Unit on its programs/projects/activities implemented.

We further recommended that management direct the officials and employees
concerned to strictly comply with the prescribed laws, rules and regulations on the
Documentary Requirements for the Evaluation of the Technical Aspect of all Government
Contracts and Inspection of Government Projects per COA Memoranda Nos. 91-704 dated
February 27, 1991 and 2005-027 dated February 28, 2005. Relative thereto, the agency must
be guided by the Checklists provided in the implementation of the future procurement
transactions of the municipality in the ensuing years. Moreover, remind the Office of the
Municipal Accountant to require the attachment of complete supporting documents on the
duly processed disbursement vouchers pertaining to procurement transactions of the
municipality, as prescribed in Section 9.0 of COA Circular No. 2012-001 dated June 14,
2012 to avoid future suspension and/or disallowance in audit.

In addition, a monthly monitoring report of the Project Status be prepared by the
local officials concerned for verification by the COA Technical Audit Specialist.

We recommended that the Management require the Municipal Ireasurer to
immediately submit the paid vouchers/payrolls accounts and issued ORs to the Office of the
Municipal Accountant for recording in the agency books of accounts, afterwhich, the same
must be submitted to the Auditor within the first ten (10) days of the ensuing month including
their supporting documents pursuant to Section 107 of P.D. 1445 and COA Circular 2009-
006.

It is suggested that the Office of the Municipal Treasurer submit the monthly accounts
on or before the fifth day of the ensuing month to the Office of the Municipal Accountant to
ensure compliance with the required deadlines.

Prompt submission of accounts and other mandated reports will facilitate fimely
review and verification of the agency’s financial transactions, thus corrective measures can
be implemented, if necessary.

We recommended to the Management strict adherence to Sections 7, 8 and 9 of COA
Circular No. 97-001 and Section 3.1.1 of CO4 Circular No. 2009-001 governing the
rendition of accounts and submission of copies of coniracts.  Further, it was suggested that
the Office of the Municipal Accountant require the attachment of complete supporting
documents on the duly processed disbursement vouchers pertaining to infrastructure projects
before effecting the payment.

We recommended that Management strictly adhere to the pertinent provisions of
COA Circular No. 2012-003 for the prevention and disallowance of irregular, unnecessary,
excessive, exiravagant and unconscionable expenditures. Compliance with the provisions on
travel law stated under Executive Order No. 298 dated March 23, 2004 and COA Circulars
No. 2012-001 dated July 14. 2012 and COA Circular No. 96-004 is required.

We recommended that the Management require the persons liable to immediately
settle the suspensions and disallowances or file an appeal memorandum with the COA
Regional Director within the appeal period of six months from receipt of the Notice of
Disallowance (ND).

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that management require the Municipal Treasurer to deposit in full the undeposited collections of P1,337,221.23 as of December 31, 2015, otherwise the same will be considered as shortage. Also, direct the Municipal Treasurer to deposit intact all his collections and compel all accountable officers/collectors to remit all their collections for the day regardless of the amount.

The Municipal Treasurer should, likewise, meticulously review the totals of the RCDs submitted by the collectors and compare the same with the total collections received per official receipts issued to ensure that all moneys received are recorded and remitted in full amount during the period.

Appropriate legal/disciplinary action should be initiated against accountable officers/collectors who continuously defy existing laws, rules and regulations on remittance or deposit of collections.
observations.managementAction: AAR 2015 - page 40-41
observations.reason: Undeposited collection amounted to P501,016.42.

observations.recommendation:
We reiterated our previous recommendation that the management of the LGU San Pascual:

a. Strictly enforce settlement of all unliquidated cash advances as of December 31,2015.

b. Stop the granting of additional cash advances to officers and employees unless the previous ones are fully settled/liquidated to avoid accumulation of unliquidated cash advances.

Send demand letters to all concerned requiring the immediate settlement and/or refund of the amount advanced, and enforce the withholding of salaries of those who fail to settle/liquidate their cash advances within the prescribed period after the demand.

Initiate appropriate legal/disciplinary action against those erring accountable officers for continued non-compliance with the existing laws and regulations, if warranted.
observations.managementAction: AAR 2015 - page 41-44
observations.reason: Unliquidated cash advances previous years amounted to P1,978,965.28. Please see related Audit Observation No.2.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that Management direct the Municipal Planning and Development Coordinator (MPDC) and Municipal Engineer to ensure that the 20% EDF is optimally utilized in the prompt implementation of programs and projects to achieve the desirable socio-economic development for the Municipality’s constituents. Further, strict observance of timetable for the implementation of specific phase of activities to be undertaken will aid the agency in prioritizing activities to be implemented given the available resources for that period to achieve results.
